cards.title: Tired of AI Noise? People Are Paying for Human-Curated, Private Digital Spaces
cards.thesis: People are getting seriously fed up with the flood of AI-generated content online and are actively seeking ways to filter it out and find authentic, human-created experiences. At the same time, they're worried about privacy when using AI tools, creating a strong demand for solutions that give them control over their AI exposure and data.

evidence.text: There's massive interest in adding a flag for AI-generated articles on Hacker News, not to de-rank them, but to allow users who 'don't like reading AI-generated text, to skip it.' This post alone got 1487 engagements.

People are asking 'How do you use LLMs for private discussions?' and noting that trying to keep conversations with AI private (using Tor, random accounts, untraceable payments) 'seems like a lot of effort,' highlighting a friction point.
A user asked if AI has 'changed the quality of HN posts,' admitting they 'thought AI-generated content would making online discussion worse,' showing a clear concern about quality degradation.
There's a noticeable trend of people sharing and appreciating 'personal websites,' indicating a desire for unique, human-centric online spaces over generic content.
Builders are actively asking 'Anyone building something in the AI space?' and 'What are you working on?' (1246 engagement), showing a strong appetite to create solutions within the AI ecosystem.

cards.opportunity: Right now, everyone's either building AI or complaining about AI-generated content fatigue, but few are building tools that give users *personal control* over their AI experience. You could build a browser extension or a lightweight desktop app that lets users set their own 'AI boundaries' – imagine a 'Do Not Disturb' for AI, where you can hide AI-generated content on specific sites, or automatically anonymize your LLM chats to protect privacy. This isn't just about detection; it's about empowering users to curate their own digital world and protect their data, and you could ship a basic, customizable MVP this weekend using open-source AI content flags and a simple proxy for LLM calls.
